senses.definition: A rare or obsolete spelling or form of the word "combination" meaning a mixture or joining of things.
examples: The author used the word "accombination" in an old text to describe a joining of forces.
In historical documents, "accombination" may appear where modern English uses "combination".

senses.definition: Able to be accommodated, adjusted to, or provided with what is needed.
examples: The hall is accommodable for up to 300 guests.
We only accept projects that are easily accommodable within our schedule.

senses.definition: The quality of being willing or able to accommodate, help, or adapt to others.
examples: Her accommodableness made her popular with both clients and colleagues.
The accommodableness of the staff contributed to the hotel’s good reputation.

senses.definition: To provide someone with a place to live, stay, or sit.
examples: The hotel can accommodate up to 500 guests.
The stadium was built to accommodate large crowds.

senses.definition: To change something so that it fits the needs, wishes, or situation of someone or something.
examples: The schedule was changed to accommodate working parents.
The software can be adjusted to accommodate different user needs.

senses.definition: To help someone by doing what they want or by providing what they ask for.
examples: We always try to accommodate our clients’ special requests.
The manager was happy to accommodate her need for flexible hours.

senses.definition: Provided with somewhere to live, stay, or sit.
examples: All conference participants are accommodated in nearby hotels.
The refugees were temporarily accommodated in a sports hall.

senses.definition: Having one’s needs or wishes met or adjusted for.
examples: Employees felt well accommodated under the new policy.
Dietary restrictions can be accommodated on request.

senses.definition: In a way that accommodates or is willing to help or adapt.
examples: He responded accommodately to their last-minute changes.
The team worked accommodately with the new partners.

senses.definition: The state of being appropriate, suitable, or fitting to a need or situation.
examples: Critics praised the accommodateness of the solution to local needs.
The accommodateness of the venue impressed the organizers.

senses.definition: Third person singular of "accommodate": provides space or meets needs or wishes.
examples: The hotel accommodates both business and leisure travelers.
The new policy accommodates different working styles.

senses.definition: Willing to help or do things for others; easy to work with.
examples: The staff were very accommodating during our stay.
She is always accommodating when colleagues need help.

senses.definition: In a helpful or cooperative way, especially by changing to suit others.
examples: The manager accommodatingly agreed to change the schedule.
She smiled accommodatingly and offered them another option.

senses.definition: The quality of being willing to help, cooperate, or adapt to others.
examples: His accommodatingness made him a valued team member.
The business is known for its accommodatingness toward small clients.

senses.definition: A place where you live or stay, especially on holiday or for a short time.
examples: We are still looking for accommodation near the city center.
Our accommodation was a small guesthouse by the sea.

senses.definition: An agreement or arrangement between people or groups who have different opinions.
examples: The two sides finally reached an accommodation after long talks.
They are trying to find an accommodation between tradition and modern life.

senses.definition: The process of adapting or adjusting to something.
examples: Cultural accommodation can be challenging for new immigrants.
The company’s rapid accommodation to market changes impressed analysts.

senses.definition: The automatic adjustment of the eye to focus on objects at different distances.
examples: Accommodation of the eye decreases with age.
The optician explained how accommodation works when we focus on near objects.

senses.definition: Relating to accommodation, especially the adjustment of the eye for seeing at different distances.
examples: The patient showed reduced accommodational ability in both eyes.
Accommodational changes become noticeable in middle age.

senses.definition: Someone who tries to adapt to or compromise with an opposing group or opinion, often to avoid conflict.
examples: Critics called him an accommodationist for working with the ruling party.
Some activists accused the leadership of being too accommodationist toward their opponents.

senses.definition: Describing a person or attitude that favors compromise or adaptation to an opposing view.
examples: The government adopted an accommodationist approach to religious groups.
Some scholars defend an accommodationist interpretation of the law on church and state.

senses.definition: Plural of "accommodationist": people who try to adapt to or compromise with an opposing group or opinion.
examples: Accommodationists argued that limited compromise was better than no deal.
The movement split between radicals and accommodationists.

senses.definition: Places where people live or stay, such as rooms, hotels, or cabins.
examples: We booked our accommodations through a travel website.
The ship offers luxury accommodations for its passengers.

senses.definition: Adjustments or changes made so that something is easier for someone, especially people with special needs.
examples: The school provides accommodations for students with disabilities.
Reasonable accommodations must be made for employees who need them.

senses.definition: Willing to help or adapt to what someone else wants or needs.
examples: Her accommodative attitude made negotiations easier.
The company has been very accommodative toward flexible work requests.

senses.definition: Describing a central bank or economic policy that makes it easier to borrow money, usually by keeping interest rates low.
examples: The central bank maintained an accommodative monetary policy to support growth.
Investors welcomed the accommodative stance of the authorities.

senses.definition: In a way that is willing to help or adapt to others’ needs, or in a way that supports economic activity by making credit easier.
examples: He responded accommodatively to all their concerns.
The central bank acted accommodatively to prevent a downturn.

senses.definition: The quality of being willing to help or adapt to what others want.
examples: Her accommodativeness contributed greatly to the project’s success.
The accommodativeness of local officials surprised the journalists.

senses.definition: Plural of "accommodativeness": different kinds or instances of being willing to help or adapt to others’ needs.
examples: Different cultural accommodativenesses can affect negotiations.
The study compared the accommodativenesses of various organizations.

senses.definition: A person who accommodates others, especially someone who is ready to help, adapt, or compromise.
examples: As an accommodator, she tried to keep peace within the team.
Negotiations often require at least one accommodator at the table.

senses.definition: Plural of "accommodator": people who are ready to help, adapt, or compromise.
examples: In any conflict, accommodators play a key role in reaching agreement.
The committee included both hardliners and accommodators.

senses.definition: Common misspelling of "accommodate" meaning to provide space or meet someone’s needs.
examples: Many people mistakenly write "accomodate" instead of "accommodate".
Spellcheckers usually correct "accomodate" to "accommodate" automatically.

senses.definition: Able to be accompanied or suitable to be accompanied by something or someone.
examples: The song is easily accompanable on piano.
This dish is highly accompanable with a light white wine.

senses.definition: Together with another person or thing; having someone or something with you.
examples: Children under 12 must be accompanied by an adult.
She arrived accompanied by her lawyer.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of "accompany": went or was together with someone or something.
examples: She accompanied her friend to the doctor.
The singer was accompanied by a pianist.

senses.definition: Someone who accompanies another person; a companion or escort.
examples: Each child must have an accompanier during the trip.
The museum offers free entry for an accompanier of a disabled visitor.

senses.definition: Plural of "accompanier": people who go with or escort others.
examples: All accompaniers must register at the front desk.
The school provided information for parents and other accompaniers.

senses.definition: Third person singular of "accompany": goes or is together with someone or something.
examples: She usually accompanies her grandfather to the market.
A high fever often accompanies this illness.

senses.definition: Music that is played to support a singer or another instrument.
examples: She sang a folk song with piano accompaniment.
The violin provides a soft accompaniment to the main melody.

senses.definition: Something that is served or happens together with something else.
examples: We served grilled fish with a salad as an accompaniment.
Fear was a constant accompaniment to their journey.

senses.definition: Relating to or serving as an accompaniment in music.
examples: The piece contains complex accompanimental figures for the piano.
Her accompanimental role was essential to the overall sound.

senses.definition: Plural of "accompaniment": musical parts that support a main performer.
examples: The choir sang with simple guitar accompaniments.
Instrumental accompaniments were added in the new arrangement.

senses.definition: Things that are served or used together with something else.
examples: The main dish comes with various accompaniments such as rice and vegetables.
Sauces and other accompaniments were placed on the table.

senses.definition: A musician, often a pianist, who plays music to support a singer or another musician.
examples: The singer thanked her accompanist at the end of the concert.
He works as a piano accompanist for the choir.

senses.definition: Plural of "accompanist": musicians who play to support singers or other performers.
examples: The choir employs several accompanists for different performances.
Accompanists must listen carefully to follow the soloist.

senses.definition: To go somewhere with someone.
examples: I will accompany you to the airport.
Children under 16 must be accompanied by an adult.

senses.definition: To happen or exist at the same time as something else.
examples: High unemployment is often accompanied by social problems.
His speech was accompanied by loud applause.

senses.definition: To play music to support a singer or another instrument.
examples: She will accompany the singer on the piano.
A small orchestra accompanied the soloist.

senses.definition: Happening or existing together with something else.
examples: We discussed the main report and the accompanying documents.
She felt nervous and had the accompanying symptoms of sweating and shaking.

senses.definition: Present participle of "accompany": going with someone or happening together with something.
examples: She is accompanying her boss on a business trip.
The rise in prices is accompanying a fall in demand.

senses.definition: Nonstandard or rare form of "accompanist": a musician who plays music to support a singer or another performer.
examples: He worked as an accompanyist for local singers.
The term "accompanyist" is sometimes used instead of "accompanist".

senses.definition: Plural of "accompanyist": musicians who support singers or other performers, usually called accompanists.
examples: Several accompanyists volunteered for the student concert.
The word "accompanyists" is far less common than "accompanists".

senses.definition: A very rare term meaning something that complements or accompanies something else.
examples: In that old treatise, "accomplement" refers to a fitting counterpart.
Scholars noted the unusual use of "accomplement" in the text.

senses.definition: Relating to completing or perfecting something; serving as a complement or completion.
examples: The final chapter plays an accompletive role in the argument.
These data have an accompletive function in the overall analysis.

senses.definition: A completed and often irreversible action or fact, usually in the phrase "fait accompli".
examples: By the time we were consulted, the merger was already a fait accompli.
The decision was presented to staff as an accompli.

senses.definition: A person who helps someone else commit a crime or do something wrong.
examples: The thief and his accomplice were both arrested.
She denied being an accomplice to the fraud.

senses.definition: Plural of "accomplice": people who help someone commit a crime or do something wrong.
examples: Police are still searching for his accomplices.
The judge said the accomplices were equally responsible.

senses.definition: The state of being an accomplice; involvement in wrongdoing with others.
examples: He was accused of accompliceship in the robbery.
The court examined her possible accompliceship in the crime.

senses.definition: Involvement with another person in a crime or in doing something wrong.
examples: The investigation focused on his accomplicity in the fraud.
She denied any accomplicity in the scandal.

senses.definition: A French-derived form related to "accomplice" or "accompli", mainly seen in fixed phrases or foreign-language contexts.
examples: The phrase included the French word "accomplis".
In that novel, "accomplis" appears as part of a French expression.

senses.definition: To succeed in doing or finishing something, especially something important.
examples: She accomplished all her goals for the year.
It will not be easy to accomplish this task.

senses.definition: Able to be done or achieved.
examples: With enough time, this plan is definitely accomplishable.
Set realistic and accomplishable targets for your team.

senses.definition: Very skilled at something.
examples: She is an accomplished pianist.
The course is taught by an accomplished scientist.

senses.definition: Successfully completed or achieved.
examples: With this step, the project is now accomplished.
They celebrated their accomplished mission.

senses.definition: A person who accomplishes something; someone who achieves goals or tasks.
examples: He is a quiet accomplisher who rarely talks about his success.
Effective leaders are often great accomplishers of difficult tasks.

senses.definition: Plural of "accomplisher": people who successfully achieve goals or tasks.
examples: The team is full of strong accomplishers.
Organizations need both dreamers and accomplishers.
